frontier_justification: This direction remains a May 2026 frontier because fusion control has shown repeated breakthroughs with deep learning and reinforcement learning, but existing results are device-specific and phase-limited. The unresolved step is a transferable, safety-constrained control model that combines simulators, multi-tokamak data, and experimental adaptation on the path toward ITER-scale operation.

papers.title: Predicting disruptive instabilities in controlled fusion plasmas through deep learning
papers.key_contribution: First deep learning system for cross-machine disruption prediction in tokamak fusion plasmas; trained on DIII-D and JET experimental data, the model reliably forecasts disruptions even on machines it was not trained on — a crucial capability for ITER and future reactors that cannot afford training disruptions.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Plasma disruptions — sudden loss of confinement — are the most dangerous events in tokamak fusion reactors, potentially damaging the vessel; predicting them in advance is critical but existing methods fail to transfer across different tokamak machines.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Deep recurrent neural network (LSTM/CNN) trained on time-series diagnostic data from DIII-D (US) and JET (EU) tokamaks; multi-signal input from hundreds of plasma diagnostics; binary classification of disruptive vs. non-disruptive trajectories with configurable warning time.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves reliable disruption prediction on both DIII-D and JET; critically, the model trained on one machine transfers to another without retraining, demonstrating cross-machine generalization essential for ITER deployment.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Purely predictive (passive monitoring): predicts disruptions but cannot prevent them; no control action is taken; requires extensive diagnostic data infrastructure; the binary prediction (disruptive/non-disruptive) does not identify the physical cause or suggest mitigation strategies.

papers.title: Machine learning for disruption warnings on Alcator C-Mod, DIII-D, and EAST
papers.key_contribution: Random forest-based disruption warning system deployed in real-time on three tokamaks (Alcator C-Mod, DIII-D, EAST); achieves 80-90% disruption detection rates using only real-time-available plasma parameters, with successful inter-shot and real-time operation — the first ML disruption predictor validated across three distinct machines simultaneously.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Kates-Harbeck 2019 demonstrated cross-machine disruption prediction with deep learning but used complex recurrent networks requiring extensive diagnostic data; a simpler, more interpretable approach using only real-time plasma parameters could enable practical deployment on diverse tokamaks including EAST (China).
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Random forest classifier trained on large databases of plasma parameters sampled approximately 10^6 times across 10^4 discharges from three tokamaks; uses only parameters available in real-time (no post-shot diagnostics); binary classification of time slices as 'close to disruption' vs. 'far from disruption'.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves 80-90% disruption detection rates on all three machines on a shot-by-shot basis; demonstrates successful real-time deployment on EAST and DIII-D; the simpler random forest approach proves that practical disruption warning does not require deep learning complexity.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Random forest is a shallow model with limited ability to capture temporal dynamics; the binary classification framework does not provide graded warning or identify disruption causes; performance varies substantially across machines on a time-slice basis.

papers.title: Magnetic control of tokamak plasmas through deep reinforcement learning
papers.key_contribution: First deep reinforcement learning system deployed for real-time plasma shape control on a tokamak (TCV at EPFL); the RL agent adjusts 19 magnetic coil voltages 10,000 times per second based on 90 diagnostic measurements, producing diverse plasma configurations including advanced shapes and dual droplets.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Kates-Harbeck 2019 and Montes 2019 demonstrated that machine learning can predict disruptions, but passive prediction alone cannot prevent them; active control of plasma shape and position through magnetic coils requires real-time decision-making that traditional PID controllers handle poorly for complex configurations.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Deep RL agent (MPO algorithm) trained in a high-fidelity tokamak simulator; at deployment, the neural network takes 90 real-time diagnostic measurements as input and outputs voltage commands to 19 magnetic coils at 10 kHz; trained to produce target plasma shapes via reward shaping.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Successfully controls diverse plasma configurations on TCV including elongated shapes, negative triangularity, snowflake divertor, and sustained dual droplets (two simultaneous plasma volumes); first demonstration of RL for real-time tokamak control.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Trained and deployed on a single tokamak (TCV); general plasma shape control but no instability-specific avoidance; the RL agent cannot handle tearing modes or other MHD instabilities that require anticipatory control; requires accurate simulator for training.

papers.title: Offline Model-Based Reinforcement Learning for Tokamak Control
papers.key_contribution: Offline model-based RL for tokamak control: learns a dynamics model from historical DIII-D experimental data and trains an RL policy entirely offline without requiring a physics simulator; successfully tested for both feedforward trajectory design and feedback control on DIII-D, demonstrating that RL can work with offline data alone.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Degrave 2022's RL agent required a high-fidelity physics simulator for training, which is expensive to develop for each new tokamak; an offline approach that learns dynamics models directly from historical experimental data would bypass the need for simulation and enable faster deployment.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Neural network dynamics model trained on historical DIII-D discharge data; the model serves as a surrogate environment for offline RL policy training; combines model-based planning with reinforcement learning; policy is deployed for real-time feedback control without requiring online interaction during training.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Successfully designs feedforward trajectories and performs feedback control on DIII-D using a policy trained entirely offline on historical data; demonstrates that the sim-to-real gap can be bypassed by learning directly from experimental data.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: The offline dynamics model's accuracy is limited by the coverage of historical data; the approach does not explicitly handle instabilities or disruptions; validated only for basic control tasks (current and shape tracking), not for instability avoidance.

papers.title: Avoiding fusion plasma tearing instability with deep reinforcement learning
papers.key_contribution: Deep RL agent that actively avoids tearing instabilities — the leading cause of plasma disruptions — on DIII-D tokamak; the agent navigates high-pressure plasma through safe operational paths in real time, maintaining H-mode performance while keeping tearing likelihood below a controllable threshold.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Degrave 2022 demonstrated RL for general plasma shape control but did not address MHD instabilities; Char 2023 showed offline RL is feasible but without instability awareness; tearing instability is the leading cause of disruptions in ITER-relevant scenarios, and avoiding it requires anticipatory control that navigates through a time-varying stability boundary.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: RL agent trained on a data-driven dynamics model of DIII-D plasma; real-time tearing instability likelihood is estimated and used as a constraint; the agent adjusts plasma parameters (current, pressure profile) to keep tearing likelihood below threshold while maintaining high confinement (H-mode).
idea_genome.observation_genome: Successfully maintains tearing likelihood below threshold on DIII-D even under unfavorable conditions (low safety factor, low torque); the RL-controlled plasma actively tracks stable paths within the time-varying operational space while sustaining H-mode performance.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Trained and validated on a single tokamak (DIII-D); the data-driven dynamics model requires extensive experimental data for each new machine; limited to tearing instability (does not handle other MHD modes); real-time inference must meet strict latency requirements.

papers.title: TORAX: A Fast and Differentiable Tokamak Transport Simulator in JAX
papers.key_contribution: TORAX: open-source differentiable tokamak transport simulator built in JAX; solves coupled equations for ion/electron heat transport, particle transport, and current diffusion with automatic differentiation, enabling gradient-based optimization of plasma scenarios and seamless integration with ML surrogate models.

idea_genome.problem_genome: RL-based plasma control (Degrave 2022, Seo 2024) relies on either physics simulators or data-driven models as training environments, but neither supports gradient-based optimization; a differentiable physics simulator would enable direct gradient computation through the plasma dynamics, unlocking new optimization and ML integration strategies.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Implements tokamak core transport equations in JAX, leveraging just-in-time compilation for speed and automatic differentiation for gradient computation; modular architecture allows swapping physics-based transport models with ML surrogates; validated against the established RAPTOR code.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves fast simulation times with JIT compilation; automatic differentiation enables gradient-based scenario optimization impossible with traditional simulators; validated against RAPTOR with close agreement on plasma profiles; the open-source JAX implementation facilitates ML community adoption.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Currently focused on 1D core transport (no edge/SOL physics); the differentiable framework has not yet been integrated with RL-based control agents; validated for standard tokamak scenarios but not yet for extreme or disruption-prone regimes.

papers.title: High-fidelity data-driven dynamics model for reinforcement learning-based control in HL-3 tokamak
papers.key_contribution: High-fidelity data-driven dynamics model enables RL-based plasma control on China's HL-3 tokamak; the learned dynamics model serves as a training environment for RL, achieving stable 400-ms, 1-kHz trajectory control with accurate waveform tracking of plasma current and last closed flux surface.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Seo 2024 used a data-driven model for DIII-D but building such models for new tokamaks (like China's HL-3) is challenging; Char 2023's offline approach showed feasibility but needs adaptation; the dynamics model must be high-fidelity enough to serve as an RL training environment while being learnable from limited experimental data.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Data-driven neural network dynamics model trained on HL-3 experimental discharge data; the model predicts plasma state evolution (current, shape, flux surface) given actuator commands; RL agent trained entirely in this learned simulator before deployment on the real tokamak.
idea_genome.observation_genome: RL agent trained in the learned dynamics model successfully maintains stable 400-ms plasma operation on HL-3 at 1 kHz control frequency; achieves accurate waveform tracking of plasma current and last closed flux surface; demonstrates transferability of the data-driven RL paradigm to a new tokamak.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Validated for relatively short plasma durations (400 ms); the data-driven model requires sufficient experimental data from HL-3; limited to basic shape/current control without instability-specific avoidance; the learned dynamics model's accuracy degrades for plasma regimes far from training distribution.

papers.title: Learning plasma dynamics and robust rampdown trajectories with predict-first experiments at TCV
papers.key_contribution: Neural state-space model (NSSM) combined with RL for robust plasma rampdown trajectory design at TCV; the NSSM learns plasma dynamics from only 311 pulses (5 in high-performance regime), and RL optimizes trajectories that avoid instability limits; predict-first experiments demonstrate 20% plasma current increase from baseline.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Wu 2025 demonstrated data-driven dynamics + RL on HL-3 but required substantial experimental data; plasma rampdown (safe termination) is a critical operational phase where instabilities frequently occur; a data-efficient approach that works with limited high-performance shots is needed.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Neural state-space model (NSSM) from Scientific Machine Learning combines physics priors with neural network flexibility; learns plasma dynamics from only 311 TCV discharges; RL parallelized across model uncertainties designs trajectories avoiding instability limits; predict-first experimental validation on TCV.
idea_genome.observation_genome: The NSSM-RL system achieves statistically significant improvements in rampdown metrics; predict-first experiment increases plasma current by 20% from baseline; demonstrates that data-efficient SciML can enable plasma control with minimal experimental data in reactor-relevant regimes.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Limited to plasma rampdown phase (not full discharge control); NSSM extrapolation ability constrained to small deviations from training regime; validated on TCV which is smaller than reactor-scale devices; integration with instability-specific avoidance (like Seo 2024) remains unexplored.

edges.evolution_focus: Montes 2019 competes directly with Kates-Harbeck 2019 in the disruption prediction niche, offering a simpler alternative (random forest vs. deep LSTM/CNN) that achieves comparable disruption detection rates across three tokamaks using only real-time plasma parameters — demonstrating that practical deployment does not require deep learning complexity.

Deep learning radiates from passive disruption prediction (Kates-Harbeck's classification model) to active real-time plasma control (Degrave's RL agent) — the same deep learning toolkit applied to a fundamentally new problem: from forecasting instabilities to directly commanding magnetic coils to shape plasma.

Char 2023 refines Degrave's RL-for-tokamak paradigm by replacing the physics simulator with an offline data-driven dynamics model, eliminating the need for an expensive physics simulation environment — an incremental but important mechanism improvement that makes the RL approach more practical for diverse tokamaks.

Seo's RL controller specializes from Degrave's general-purpose plasma shape control to instability-specific avoidance, splitting the RL-for-tokamak lineage into two distinct niches: general shape control (Degrave) and targeted disruption avoidance (Seo) requiring fundamentally different reward structures and dynamics models.

TORAX hybridizes the differentiable programming paradigm from modern ML frameworks (JAX) with traditional physics-based tokamak transport simulation, creating a simulator that supports both automatic differentiation for gradient-based optimization and modular ML surrogate integration — combining Degrave's DeepMind ML expertise with fusion physics.

Wu adapts the data-driven RL paradigm from Seo's DIII-D work to an entirely new device (China's HL-3 tokamak), radiating the approach to a different machine with different plasma parameters, diagnostics, and operating regimes — demonstrating the technique's portability across international fusion programs.

The TCV rampdown work refines the data-driven dynamics + RL paradigm (from Wu/HL-3) by introducing a neural state-space model for greater data efficiency (311 pulses vs. extensive datasets), adding predict-first experimental validation, and targeting the critical rampdown operational phase.

open_question: Predict-first TCV and reinforcement-learning plasma-control studies show that data-driven control can learn device-specific dynamics, but ITER-scale deployment needs cross-device transfer, uncertainty-aware safety, and simulator-to-shot adaptation. What fusion-control foundation model would combine differentiable plasma simulators, multi-tokamak logs, and constrained RL to produce robust ramp-up, sustainment, and ramp-down policies across machines?

question.name: Question
question.description: Present only the domain/subfield and the curated May-2026 research question; ask the model to propose a next-step research direction in Plasma Physics / AI-Driven Fusion Control.

library.name: Library
library.description: Present the curated research frontier plus 8 trace papers in random order with paper titles and brief abstracts, but without explicit lineage order, genome fields, or dynamics labels; ask the model to propose the next research direction.

lineage.name: Lineage
lineage.description: Present the curated research frontier plus the ordered trace with genome metadata, GenomeDiff-style change evidence, and parent-child relationships; ask the model to predict the next evolutionary step without naming predefined category labels.

frontier_evidence.title: Learning plasma dynamics and robust rampdown trajectories with predict-first experiments at TCV
frontier_evidence.unresolved_gap: Data-driven plasma control works on specific tokamaks and phases, but reactor-relevant deployment still needs cross-device transfer, uncertainty-aware safety, and robust control across full discharge phases.
frontier_evidence.why_frontier: Used as the May 2026 frontier anchor: the arena question targets a still-open limitation at the endpoint of this lineage, not a solved benchmark task.

frontier_status.manual_review: Re-audited on 2026-05-05 for appendix release: active arena trace has a 2025/2026 frontier endpoint, a non-solved open research question, valid forward lineage edges, and generation settings aligned with post-hoc dynamics evaluation.
frontier_status.prompt_role: The same research question is shown in Question, Library, and Lineage settings; only the supporting literature/lineage context changes.
